sanskrit_devanagari:
श्रीभगवानुवाच
बहूनि मे व्यतीतानि जन्मानि तव चार्जुन ।
तान्यहं वेद सर्वाणि न त्वं वेत्थ परन्तप ॥ ५ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
śrī-bhagavān uvāca
bahūni me vyatītāni janmāni tava cārjuna |
tāny ahaṁ veda sarvāṇi na tvaṁ vettha paran-tapa || 5 ||

translations.verbatim_quote: The Personality of Godhead said: Many, many births both you and I have passed. I can remember all of them, but you cannot, O subduer of the enemy!
The Personality of Godhead said: Many, many births both you and I have passed. I can remember all of them, but you cannot, O subduer of the enemy!

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use excerpt; full purport length: 3940 chars)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: In the Brahma-saṁhitā (5.33) we have information of many, many incarnations of the Lord. It is stated there:advaitam acyutam anādim ananta-rūpamādyaṁ purāṇa-puruṣaṁ nava-yauvanaṁ cavedeṣu durlabham adurlabham ātma-bhaktaugovindam ādi-puruṣaṁ tam ahaṁ bhajāmi“I worship the Supreme Personality of Godh…

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 4.5 O Arjuna, bahuni, many; janmani, lives; me, of Mine; vyatitani, have passed; tava ca, and so have yours. Aham, I; veda know; tani, them; sarvani, all; (but) tvam, you; va vetta, know not, due to your power of understanding being obstructed by righteousness, unrighteousness, etc.
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-4-5-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Adi Shankaracharya of Advaita Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads the verse as Krishna establishing that ordinary jīva consciousness loses awareness across births, whereas the Lord retains full knowledge of his successive births because his nature is unobscured by māyā. The asymmetry lies in unobstructed self-knowledge.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 4.5 The Lord said — By this, the reality of the Lord’s birth is declared in the sentence, ‘Many births of Mine have passed.’ ‘So is it with you’ is added by way of illustration. The mode of incarnation, the reality of His body and the cause of His birth are explained in the following verse.
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-4-5-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Ramanuja of Sri Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja reads the verse as Krishna's claim that he is sarvajña (all-knowing) — knowing his own previous births and those of all beings — while Arjuna, being an embodied jīva, does not have this knowledge. The verse establishes Krishna's omniscience without yet naming the avatar mechanism.

positions.rendering: Many, many births both you and I have passed. I can remember all of them, but you cannot, O subduer of the enemy!
Krishna retains unobscured self-knowledge across births; Arjuna's jīva consciousness is veiled by māyā and cannot recall
Krishna is sarvajña (omniscient over all beings' births); Arjuna's knowledge is bounded by jīva-level cognition

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: Prabhupada reads 'veda' simply as 'remember.' Shankara emphasises the metaphysical mechanism (māyā veils the jīva but not the Lord). Ramanuja emphasises the scope (Krishna knows all beings' births, not just his own). The three readings together preserve the asymmetry the verse establishes without flattening it into ordinary memory.

literal_meaning: The Lord said: Many of my births have passed, and yours too, Arjuna; I know all of them, you do not, scorcher of foes.
traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna answers Arjuna's chronological question by declaring an asymmetry: both have had many births, but only Krishna remembers them. The verse sets up the avatar doctrine without yet stating it: the difference between Krishna and Arjuna is not that one has been born and the other has not; it is that one knows all his births and the other does not. The verse foregrounds knowledge as the discriminator.
